feed.title: A Parent's Guide to AI - General

entry.title: AI Scams Targeting Families: Voice Cloning, Fake Emergencies, and What to Do
entry.summary: AI can now clone voices from a short audio clip. Scammers are using this to impersonate children in distress and convince grandparents to wire money. Here's what these scams look like and how to protect your family.
entry.content:
Most of the conversations in this blog and in the parent's guide focus on how AI affects children. This post is about how AI is being used to target families (including the adults in them).

AI-powered scams targeting families are not theoretical. They are happening now, in Canada, including in BC. They have successfully defrauded real people out of real money, and they have caused significant distress to families who discovered that the panicked call from their "child" was staged by a machine.

This is worth understanding before it happens to you.

What voice cloning actually is

Modern AI systems can clone a person's voice from a short audio sample: in some cases, as little as three to five seconds of recorded speech.1 The cloned voice can then say anything.

The output is not a recording. It is a generated audio file that sounds like the person speaking words they never said. The quality varies, but in casual phone-call conditions (not side-by-side comparison with the real person), it can be convincing enough to deceive people who know the target's voice well.2

The audio samples needed to create a clone are available for almost any teenager or adult who has posted videos on social media, left voicemails, or appeared in video calls. Your child's voice may already exist in enough publicly available material to clone.

The grandparent scam, AI-powered

The "grandparent scam" has existed for years: a caller impersonates a grandchild in distress ("Grandma, I'm in trouble, don't tell Mom and Dad") and asks for money urgently, often through wire transfer or gift cards.

AI voice cloning has made this scam significantly more effective.

A scammer can now call a grandparent using a voice that sounds like their actual grandchild, not a generic young voice. They can combine the voice clone with information gathered from social media (the grandchild's name, details about their life, recent events) to add convincing detail. The caller may then hand the phone to a fake "lawyer" or "police officer" who explains what the grandchild needs.

This works because the emotional urgency of the call makes careful evaluation harder. A grandparent who hears their grandchild's voice and genuine-sounding distress activates a protective response, not a fraud-detection response.

The Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre has documented these calls in Canada, including cases where amounts in the tens of thousands of dollars were transferred before the fraud was discovered.3

The fake emergency call

A related scam targets parents directly. A caller impersonates a child's voice to say they've been in an accident, arrested, or kidnapped. In the kidnapping variation (sometimes called a "virtual kidnapping" scam), parents hear the child screaming or crying, and then the call is taken over by a scammer making demands.

These calls are designed to be short and panicked, to prevent the target from hanging up and calling back. The scammer keeps the target on the line, building emotional urgency while a second scammer instructs them on transferring money.

The detail that makes AI voice cloning particularly effective here is that even a parent who is initially skeptical may be convinced when the voice sounds genuinely like their child. The threshold for "this might be real" goes up when the voice is familiar.

Phishing, impersonation, and AI-generated text

Voice cloning is the most dramatic example, but AI is also being used to improve traditional scams that target families.

AI-generated phishing messages are harder to detect than older phishing. Historically, phishing emails contained spelling errors and grammatical mistakes. AI-generated phishing produces grammatically correct, contextually appropriate messages that can be personalized to the target: referencing their school, their employer, their neighbourhood.

Impersonation of school officials or school boards is a documented scam type. A message that appears to be from your child's principal or from the school district, with appropriate official language, requesting payment for a trip deposit or explaining that your child is in a situation requiring your immediate attention.

AI-generated fake invoices and utility notices increasingly target families. A fake notice from a recognizable utility or government agency (with appropriate formatting and language) is harder to dismiss than older scams.

How to protect your family

Establish a code word

This is the most effective tool against voice cloning scams. Agree on a word or phrase (something specific that only family members know) that can be used in a call to verify identity. A person in genuine distress can say the code word. An AI clone cannot, because it doesn't know it.

Establish this with your children, your parents, and anyone who might receive a distress call related to your family. Review it periodically in case it's forgotten.

Call back on a known number

If you receive a call claiming your child is in trouble, hang up and call your child's actual phone. Call the school. Call the hospital or police station the caller mentioned and verify the situation independently. A real emergency will survive a two-minute verification call. A scam will not.

Do not call back the number that called you. Use a number you already have.

Slow down on any request for money

The common denominator in almost every AI-assisted scam is urgency: "act now, don't tell anyone, the window will close." This urgency is a manipulation tool. Any legitimate emergency involving your family can withstand you saying "I need to make one call to verify this before I do anything."

Scammers will push back on this. A legitimate caller will not.

Limit the audio samples available publicly

If your child appears in videos on social media (their own account or others'), those videos provide voice samples that could theoretically be used for cloning. This is not a reason to eliminate your child's social media presence, but it is worth factoring into decisions about what gets posted.

Private accounts reduce but don't eliminate this risk. Content shared in private messages can be screenshotted or recorded.

Talk to grandparents and older relatives

Older adults are disproportionately targeted by grandparent scams. If your children have grandparents or older relatives, a direct conversation about this scam type (including what the code word is) is worth having. This is not about doubting their judgment. It's about giving them a tool they don't have without the conversation.

If you've been targeted

If you or a family member receives a suspicious call like the ones described here:

  • Do not send money until you have independently verified the situation.
  • Report to the Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre at antifraudcentre.ca or call 1-888-495-8501. Even if no money was lost, reporting helps track these scams and can prevent others from being victimized.
  • Report to local police if money was transferred.
  • Contact your bank immediately if a wire transfer or e-transfer was made. There may be a short window to recall it.

If you or a family member is distressed after a scam call (even if it didn't result in loss), this is a legitimate emotional response. Kids Help Phone (1-800-668-6868) is for young people. Crisis Centre BC (1-800-784-2433) serves adults.

Having the conversation with your family

The goal of this post is not to generate anxiety about every phone call. It's to give your family tools they can use before they're in a stressful situation.

Consider raising this at your next family meal, or when you're next in the car together:

  • "I've been reading about a scam where someone clones your voice using AI to trick your grandparents. I want to set up a code word we can all use."
  • "If you got a call that sounded like me but felt weird, what would you do?"
  • "What would make you suspicious that a phone call wasn't what it seemed?"

The most effective protection is a family that has talked about this before it happens, and that has a specific, shared tool (the code word) to use in the moment.

The short version:

  • AI can clone a person's voice from a short audio sample. Scammers are using this to impersonate children in distress and deceive grandparents or parents into sending money.
  • The key tactics are urgency and emotional pressure. A real emergency can survive a two-minute verification call. A scam cannot.
  • Establish a code word with your family (something only real family members know) before you need it.
  • Call back on a known number. Never call back the number that called you.
  • Report to the Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre: antifraudcentre.ca or 1-888-495-8501.

Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre: antifraudcentre-centreantifraude.ca, 1-888-495-8501

Report to local police if money was transferred.

Read the parent's guide on recognizing misinformation and staying safe online: parentsguidetoai.ca

Notes

  1. Wang, Chengyi, et al. "VALL-E: Neural Codec Language Models are Zero-Shot Text to Speech Synthesizers." Microsoft Research / arXiv, January 2023. https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.02111 ↩

  2. Kawa, Piotr, et al. "People Are Poorly Equipped to Detect AI-Powered Voice Clones." arXiv, October 2024. https://arxiv.org/html/2410.03791v1 ↩

  3. Vellani, Nelisha, and David Common. "How Con Artists Are Using AI Voice Cloning to Upgrade the Grandparent Scam." CBC Marketplace, March 20, 2025. https://www.cbc.ca/news/marketplace/marketplace-ai-voice-scam-1.7486437 — Cites Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre data showing Canadians lost nearly $3 million to the grandparent scam in 2024. ↩

entry.title: The AI Your Kids Are Already Using, Without Knowing It's AI
entry.summary: Before your child ever opens ChatGPT, they've already been shaped by AI: in their TikTok feed, their Snapchat filters, their game recommendations, and the autocomplete on their phone. Here's what that actually means.
entry.content:
When parents think about AI and children, they tend to think about ChatGPT. That's understandable. It's the most visible AI tool in public conversation right now. But by the time most children encounter a tool like ChatGPT for the first time, they've already spent years being influenced by AI systems they never recognized as AI.

The parent's guide at parentsguidetoai.ca makes this point in Section 5:

"Students often use many AI tools without realizing they are AI-powered, so parents should assume that AI is already present in: phones and tablets, search engines, Snapchat and Instagram features, TikTok recommendation systems, homework helper applications, gaming communities and Discord bots, photo editing apps, video editing software, translation tools, and browser extensions."

This post unpacks what that actually looks like, and why it matters for how you talk to your children about AI.

TikTok's recommendation engine

TikTok's "For You" page is one of the most powerful AI systems in daily use by teenagers. It is not a list of popular videos. It is a highly personalized recommendation engine that watches what you watch, how long you watch it, what you skip, whether you replay, whether you comment or share. It uses all of that to build a detailed model of what will keep you watching.1

The algorithm is genuinely impressive, which is partly what makes it concerning. It surfaces content that teenagers didn't know they wanted. Over time, it can create feedback loops. A teenager who watches a few videos about body image, or anxiety, or self-harm, can find themselves in a narrowing funnel where the algorithm keeps surfacing similar content because that's what their engagement data says they respond to.2

This is not a TikTok-specific problem. Instagram Reels and YouTube Shorts use similar systems. The underlying mechanism (AI optimization for engagement) is the same.

What's worth discussing with your child: the For You page was designed for them by an AI that has been watching them, not a neutral reflection of what's popular or true.

Snapchat filters and AI image tools

Most children using Snapchat have used its AI-powered filters: the ones that smooth skin, reshape faces, change hair colour, or add effects. It's easy to read them as fun overlays. They're actually generative AI tools applied in real time.

The implications are worth knowing:

Beauty filters alter appearance in ways tied to real psychological effects. Research has documented that prolonged use of filters that alter facial features (particularly those that smooth skin, enlarge eyes, or alter facial proportions) is associated with increased body dissatisfaction and, in some cases, a condition researchers have called "Snapchat dysmorphia": a growing trend of people seeking cosmetic procedures to match their filtered selves.3

AI image tools in these apps are learning from your child's face. Filters and "photo enhancers" in social apps typically process images on the platform's servers. The photos your child uses with these tools may contribute to training data. Terms of service vary.

Snapchat's My AI (covered briefly in Post 15 on AI companions) is embedded in the app by default. Many parents don't know it's there, and many teens don't think of it as an AI they're "using."

Gaming: AI in matchmaking, characters, and moderation

Gaming is one of the largest daily AI touchpoints for children and teenagers, and it gets almost no attention in conversations about AI literacy.

Matchmaking systems in games like Fortnite, Apex Legends, and Valorant use AI to match players of similar skill levels. In some implementations, they also aim to keep players engaged. Engagement-optimized matchmaking can mean that the system occasionally matches players against easier opponents after a losing streak, to maintain momentum and prevent them from quitting. The experience of the game (including how good your child feels about their performance) is partly being managed by an algorithm.

NPC behaviour and adaptive difficulty in single-player games use increasingly sophisticated AI. This is less concerning from a manipulation standpoint, but it's worth children understanding that the "enemy" they're fighting is a system responding to their play patterns.

Discord bots and AI features in gaming communities include everything from automated moderation (AI deciding what content gets flagged or removed) to AI-powered server tools that generate summaries, translate messages, or roleplay characters. Many Discord servers used by teenagers have AI bots running in them, often without announcement.

In-game purchase optimization is one of the more subtle applications. Some games use AI to identify players who are likely to make purchases and to tailor the experience to encourage spending, including timing offers to appear when a player is most emotionally engaged after a good result.

Google Search and AI-generated answers

Google has progressively integrated AI into search results. "AI Overviews" (the AI-generated summaries that appear at the top of many search results pages) are now a default feature of Google Search.4

This matters for children doing homework research. When a student Googles a question and reads the summary at the top of the page, they may not realize they are reading AI-generated content rather than a direct quote from a reliable source. The summary was generated by an AI synthesizing multiple sources. Like all AI-generated content, it can contain errors, oversimplifications, or missing context.

The advice in Post 5 of this series applies directly here: click through to the actual source. The AI Overview is a starting point, not the answer.

Homework apps and AI tutors

Apps like Photomath, Socratic, Khanmigo (Khan Academy's AI tutor), Grammarly, and others are AI tools designed specifically for student use. Most are legitimately useful. A few are worth knowing about in more detail.

Photomath allows students to photograph a math problem and receive a step-by-step solution. Used well, this is a learning tool: you can follow the steps and understand the method. Used carelessly, it is homework-completion software. The distinction depends entirely on whether the student is learning from the steps or just copying the answer.

Grammarly uses AI to suggest grammar, tone, and style improvements. Most schools consider this acceptable use in the same category as spell-check. But the newer versions can rewrite entire paragraphs, which may cross into territory the teacher didn't intend to allow.

AI tutoring apps vary enormously in quality. Some are genuinely excellent at adapting to a student's pace and explaining concepts clearly. Some produce confident wrong answers.

The test from the parent's guide applies here too: if your child can explain the concept without the app, they've learned something. If they can't, the app may have done the learning for them.

What to do with this information

The goal of naming these tools is not to alarm parents or prompt a device audit. It is to change the starting point of the conversation.

When you talk to your child about AI, starting from "AI is a new thing you might start using" is inaccurate and immediately signals to your child that you don't understand their world. Starting from "AI is already part of almost everything you use, including things that don't call themselves AI" is more accurate, and more likely to open a genuine conversation.

A few good conversation starters:

  • "Your TikTok For You page was built for you by an AI that's been watching what you watch. Does that seem weird to you? Does it show you things you actually like?"
  • "When you look at photos through Snapchat filters, do you think about what it would look like without the filter?"
  • "When you look something up on Google and there's a summary at the top, do you read the sources it came from?"
  • "Do you think the matchmaking in [game they play] is fair? Do you ever wonder how it decides who you're matched with?"

These are genuine questions, not gotchas. The answers will tell you what your child already understands about the AI in their daily life. That's a better foundation than starting from scratch.

The short version:

  • Most children are already using AI every day through TikTok's recommendation engine, Snapchat filters, Google AI Overviews, gaming systems, homework apps, and Discord bots.
  • Much of this AI is designed to maximize engagement, not learning. The effects of engagement-optimized AI on teenagers are genuinely uncertain.
  • Conversations about AI literacy are more credible when they start from your child's actual experience, not from ChatGPT as a new thing.
  • The AI Traffic Light from the parent's guide applies here too: understanding what kind of AI you're dealing with is the first step to using it thoughtfully.

Read the full parent's guide: parentsguidetoai.ca: Section 5, School AI vs. AI at Home.

Canada's digital literacy resource for families: MediaSmarts has specific resources on algorithmic recommendation systems and how to talk to children about them.

Notes

  1. TikTok Newsroom. "How TikTok Recommends Videos #ForYou." TikTok, June 18, 2020. https://newsroom.tiktok.com/en-us/how-tiktok-recommends-videos-for-you ↩

  2. TikTok Newsroom. "An Update on Our Work to Safeguard and Diversify Recommendations." TikTok, 2023. https://newsroom.tiktok.com/en-us/an-update-on-our-work-to-safeguard-and-diversify-recommendations ↩

  3. Rajanala, Susruthi, Mayra B.C. Maymone, and Neelam A. Vashi. "Selfies — Living in the Era of Filtered Photographs." JAMA Facial Plastic Surgery, vol. 20, no. 6, 2018. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30073294/ ↩

  4. Google. "About AI Overviews." Google Search Help, 2024. https://support.google.com/websearch/answer/14901683 ↩

entry.title: Do It By Hand First: A Method for Keeping Critical Thinking Alive in the Age of AI
entry.summary: There's a habit I've used for twenty years to learn hard things at work: solve it by hand, write down how, script the parts that repeat, and only automate once I truly understand it. It turns out that same order, do the work first and hand it off last, lines up with a lot of what we know about how durable understanding forms, and with the kind of thinking AI makes it dangerously easy to skip. Here's the method, the research behind it, and how to use it with your kids.
entry.content:
The first time I hit a genuinely hard problem at work, I solve the whole thing by hand.

I'll use every tool I have to research it: documentation, search, and yes, AI. But I make myself take each step. I want to feel where it's hard and where I was wrong. When it's finally working, I stop and write down how I actually got there, while it's fresh.

The second time the same problem shows up, I don't do it all by hand again. I take those notes and turn the boring, repeatable parts into a script (usually a small computer program), so I can spend my attention on the one or two decisions that actually need judgment.

The third time, or when I know I'll face it forever, I build something that handles it automatically. By then I understand the problem well enough to trust a machine with it. Just as importantly, I understand it well enough to notice the day the machine gets it wrong.

For years I thought this was a method for going faster. It isn't, really. It's a method for making sure that when I finally hand a task to a machine, I've already earned the understanding to catch it when it fails. And that turns out to be the exact thing AI makes it very, very easy to skip. For our kids, and for us.

This post is part method, part evidence, and part opinion, and I'll tell you which is which as we go. It is not an argument against AI; I use these tools every day, and this whole site exists to help you use them too. It's also not a research paper. It's one practical way to think about the single skill I most want my kids to keep: the ability to actually think, even when a machine offers to do it for them.

A method I borrowed from engineering

Strip the jargon off what I described above and it's a simple ladder with four rungs:

  1. Do it by hand. The first time, take every step yourself. Use tools to research, but do the actual work.
  2. Write it down. Once it works, capture how you got there, the wrong turns included.
  3. Script the parts that repeat. The second time, automate the routine steps so you can focus your attention on the parts that need real judgment.
  4. Automate it fully, but only once you've earned it. Hand the whole thing to a machine only after you understand it well enough to check its work.

The important part isn't any single rung. It's the order, and the fact that automation comes last. You don't start by handing the problem to the machine. You end there, and you only trust the handoff because you built the understanding first.

That order is the whole argument of this post. The tempting thing about AI, for a busy adult and for a twelve-year-old with homework due, is that it lets you jump straight to rung four on a problem you've never once done by hand.

Why the order matters more than the speed

Here's the part that's actually well-studied, and it surprised me when I first read it: the struggle isn't a cost you pay to get to the understanding. The struggle is how the understanding forms.

Three findings from decades of learning research point the same way.

You remember what you produce better than what you're handed. Psychologists call it the generation effect: people who generate an answer themselves, even a partial or wrong one, remember it far better than people who simply read the correct answer.1 The act of producing it is what makes it stick.

Retrieving something is a stronger teacher than reviewing it. Testing yourself on material, pulling it out of your own head, produces much more durable learning than reading it again, even though re-reading feels more productive.2 The effort of retrieval is the point.

Learning that feels harder now tends to last longer. Researchers call these desirable difficulties: conditions that slow you down and make you work often produce deeper, more durable understanding than conditions that make things feel smooth and easy.3 Easy and fast frequently means shallow and gone by next week.

I want to be careful here, because this is exactly where it's easy to overclaim. So let me under-claim instead. None of these studies measured "critical thinking" directly; they measured memory and learning. And I know of no study that has tested whether my particular four-rung method builds it. What the research does support is narrower and sturdier: understanding you build yourself sticks better than understanding you're handed.

Critical thinking leans on that, though it isn't the whole story. You can question a claim in a field you barely know, asking who's behind it, what they gain, and whether other sources agree. That's the bias-spotting habit worth teaching all on its own. But the deepest kind of check, knowing an answer is wrong because you understand the thing, only comes from having done the work. Skip the doing, and you're left with just the surface checks.

That's rung one and rung two of the ladder, and it's why they come first: do it by hand, and write it down, because that's the part that's building you.

What happens when you skip straight to the answer

Now the other side: what the research says happens when you routinely hand the thinking off before you've built it yourself.

We remember where to find things instead of the things. In a set of well-known experiments, people who expected a fact to stay available to them later remembered the fact itself less well, but remembered where to look it up better.4 This is sometimes called the "Google effect," and on its own it's not a disaster; nobody memorizes phone numbers anymore and we're fine. The trouble starts when what you've offloaded isn't a phone number but the reasoning itself.

We over-trust automation and stop catching its mistakes. Decades of research on people working alongside automated systems keeps finding the same pattern: when a machine usually gets it right, people stop watching closely and miss the errors it does make.5 And a skill you stop practicing fades. You can see a version of it in studies that link heavy reliance on turn-by-turn GPS to weaker spatial memory over time.6 The skill you don't use is the skill you lose.

And the newest research points the same direction for AI specifically. A 2025 study of knowledge workers by researchers at Microsoft and Carnegie Mellon found that the more people trusted an AI tool, the less critical thinking they reported doing. They shifted from doing the work to checking the machine's output, and sometimes skipped even that.7 A separate survey of 666 people found a similar link between frequent AI use, "cognitive offloading" (letting a tool hold the thinking for you), and lower critical-thinking scores.8

I'll be honest about how much weight those two can bear. Both are correlational, and both rest on people reporting on themselves. They can't prove which way the arrow points. It's entirely possible the causation runs backwards: the people who lean hardest on AI may already be the ones least inclined to dig in. Hold them as a strong hint, not a verdict.

You may also have seen scarier headlines about an MIT experiment that wired people up to EEG sensors while they wrote essays with ChatGPT and found weaker brain activity, the so-called "cognitive debt" study.9 I'm raising it because you've probably seen it, not because you should lean on it: it's a small, not-yet-peer-reviewed preprint that has already drawn a formal methodological critique. It's interesting; it isn't proof. And it's a tidy example of the exact habit this whole post is about. Don't bank a confident claim just because it flatters what you already suspect.

None of this says AI makes you stupid. But the honest, older, sturdier learning research and these newer hints rhyme with each other, and they rhyme with the thing this whole series keeps coming back to: AI is confident whether or not it's right, and if you've handed it the thinking, you have nothing left to catch it with.

The one rule that makes automation safe

So what's the difference between my engineering ladder and just... using AI to skip the work? On the surface they look similar. Both end with a machine doing the task.

The difference is one rule: you earn the automation, and you never fully take your hands off the wheel.

We already do this with a tool nobody panics about anymore. We let kids use calculators after they understand what multiplication is, not instead of learning it. The calculator is rung four. Arithmetic by hand is rung one. We got the order right on that one, mostly by accident and a lot of argument, and it works. A kid who understands multiplication can use a calculator all day and still notice when the screen says something absurd. A kid who never learned can't.

You could push back here, and thoughtful people do: maybe AI is simply the next calculator, and in twenty years worrying about "cognitive debt" will sound as quaint as the old panic that calculators would ruin arithmetic. That might turn out to be right for some tasks. I hold this whole argument a little loosely.

But there's a difference of scale. A calculator does one narrow thing, and you can spot-check its answer at a glance. A general-purpose AI will draft the essay, build the argument, and decide what matters. That's the whole chain of reasoning, not one arithmetic step, and it arrives in fluent, confident prose that's much harder to eyeball for "absurd." The wider the task you hand off, the more you needed to have done it yourself to judge the result. That's not an argument against the tool. It's an argument about the order.

That "still notice when it's absurd" is the part that matters, and it's a habit, not a one-time achievement. In the last post, I had an AI build a real piece of software in an evening, and the single most valuable thing I did was refuse to take its word that the work was good. I made it prove it, against a check the AI didn't control, and I kept a manual review step for myself on the one thing I actually knew how to judge. That's rung four done right: automate the work, keep your hand on the check.

The wrong version, the one to actually worry about, is handing the whole thing over on a problem you've never done, then trusting the output because it sounds confident. That's not using a tool. That's skipping straight to the top of a ladder you never climbed.

How to raise a kid who earns the shortcut

Picture the moment this actually happens. Your kid is at the kitchen table with a socials assignment: a paragraph on why the Fraser River mattered to the communities that lived along it. You watch their thumb drift toward the phone before they've written a single word. That drift is the whole ballgame.

Here's how I try to turn the ladder into something you can do in that exact moment. None of it requires you to be technical. And it scales with age: a six-year-old "doing it by hand" is sounding out a word; a sixteen-year-old's is sketching the argument before they write it. Match the ambition to the kid. An upcoming age-by-age post goes deeper on that.

Let them do it by hand first, and let it be a little hard. When a real problem shows up, whether it's that Fraser River paragraph, a tricky math question, or a text to a friend they're nervous about, the instinct is to relieve the struggle fast. Resist it for one round. The difficulty isn't the enemy of learning; it's the engine of it. Let them produce a messy first attempt before any machine touches it.

Make them show their thinking, out loud. Not "what's the answer" but "how did you get there, and where did you get stuck?" This is rung two, writing it down, in conversation form. It's also the single best defense against outsourced thinking, because a kid who can narrate their reasoning has actually done some.

Introduce AI as the second pass, not the first draft. This is the one habit I'd most want you to take from this post. Flip the usual order: do the work first, then bring in the AI to critique it, poke holes in it, or offer a version to compare against. "Here's my answer; what did I miss?" builds judgment. "Give me the answer" replaces it.

Keep one question permanently on the table: "How do you know?" Whether it's your kid or the machine talking, the reflex we're trying to build is show me how you know, not sounds right, moving on. It's the manual-review step that never goes away, no matter how good the tool gets.

Model it yourself. Kids clock what you actually do far more than what you tell them. Let them catch you doing something the slow way on purpose, like reading the article instead of the AI summary, or working out the tip without the app. Then say out loud why: because you want to keep the muscle.

This is genuinely harder than just letting the tool handle it. It's slower on a Tuesday night, and some Tuesday nights you will not have it in you. I don't either. So if you do only one thing from this list, do this: flip the order. Let them take one real swing before the machine gets a turn. Everything else here is a bonus stacked on that one habit. It's the same trade I make every time I work a problem by hand before I automate it: a little slower now, in exchange for still being the one who understands it later.

The short version:

  • I use a four-rung method to learn hard things: do it by hand, write down how, script the repeatable parts, and only fully automate once I understand it well enough to catch its mistakes. The order matters more than the speed, and automation comes last.
  • Decades of learning research back up why the doing comes first: we remember what we generate, retrieval beats re-reading, and learning that feels harder now tends to last longer.
  • Research on cognitive offloading and automation points the other way: when we hand thinking to a machine and stop checking, the underlying skill fades, and early studies suggest heavy AI reliance rhymes with less critical thinking. Most of that evidence is correlational, so hold it as a strong hint, not proof.
  • The rule that makes automation safe: earn it, and keep your hand on the wheel. We already do this with calculators: kids learn arithmetic first, so they can tell when the screen is lying.
  • With your kids: let them work the problem first, make them show their thinking, and bring AI in as the second pass, a critic, not a ghostwriter. Keep asking "how do you know?"
  • The goal isn't to keep AI away from your kids. It's to make sure they build the thinking before they hand any of it off.

Try it today: Next time your child has a question they'd normally type straight into an AI tool, ask them to take one real swing at it first, out loud or on paper, and explain their reasoning before the machine gets involved. Then look up the answer together. For yourself: pick one small thing you'd usually delegate to AI today, a summary, a draft, a decision, and do the first pass by hand before you ask the machine what it thinks. Notice which one you understand better afterward.

This is a free, independent resource with no sponsorships, referral links, or tracking. If it was useful, sharing it with another parent is the best thing you can do. Errors, outdated links, and suggestions are always welcome — open an issue on GitHub.

Notes

  1. Norman J. Slamecka and Peter Graf, "The Generation Effect: Delineation of a Phenomenon," Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Learning and Memory 4, no. 6 (1978): 592–604. https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-7393.4.6.592. Across five experiments, words participants generated themselves were remembered better than the same words simply read. ↩

  2. Henry L. Roediger III and Jeffrey D. Karpicke, "Test-Enhanced Learning: Taking Memory Tests Improves Long-Term Retention," Psychological Science 17, no. 3 (2006): 249–255. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01693.x. Students tested on a passage retained substantially more a week later than students who re-studied it, even though re-studying looked better after a few minutes. ↩

  3. Elizabeth L. Bjork and Robert A. Bjork, "Making Things Hard on Yourself, But in a Good Way: Creating Desirable Difficulties to Enhance Learning," in Psychology and the Real World: Essays Illustrating Fundamental Contributions to Society, ed. M. A. Gernsbacher, R. W. Pew, L. M. Hough, and J. R. Pomerantz (New York: Worth Publishers, 2011), 56–64. A summary chapter on the "desirable difficulties" framework rather than a single experiment. ↩

  4. Betsy Sparrow, Jenny Liu, and Daniel M. Wegner, "Google Effects on Memory: Cognitive Consequences of Having Information at Our Fingertips," Science 333, no. 6043 (2011): 776–778. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1207745. Foundational, but note that a 2018 multi-lab project failed to reproduce one of its four experiments, so treat the "Google effect" as a well-supported hypothesis rather than settled fact. ↩

  5. Raja Parasuraman and Dietrich H. Manzey, "Complacency and Bias in Human Use of Automation: An Attentional Integration," Human Factors 52, no. 3 (2010): 381–410. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720810376055. A review, not a single experiment; it integrates evidence that operators over-trust automation and miss its failures. ↩

  6. Louisa Dahmani and Véronique D. Bohbot, "Habitual Use of GPS Negatively Impacts Spatial Memory During Self-Guided Navigation," Scientific Reports 10 (2020): 6310. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-62877-0. In 50 drivers, more lifetime GPS use was associated with worse spatial memory; a three-year follow-up pointed the same way but followed only 13 people, so read that part as suggestive. ↩

  7. Hao-Ping (Hank) Lee et al., "The Impact of Generative AI on Critical Thinking: Self-Reported Reductions in Cognitive Effort and Confidence Effects From a Survey of Knowledge Workers," Proceedings of the 2025 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (2025). https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/the-impact-of-generative-ai-on-critical-thinking-self-reported-reductions-in-cognitive-effort-and-confidence-effects-from-a-survey-of-knowledge-workers/. A survey of 319 knowledge workers; self-reported and correlational. ↩

  8. Michael Gerlich, "AI Tools in Society: Impacts on Cognitive Offloading and the Future of Critical Thinking," Societies 15, no. 1 (2025): 6. https://doi.org/10.3390/soc15010006. A study of 666 participants finding a negative correlation between frequent AI use and critical-thinking scores, statistically mediated by cognitive offloading. Correlational; it cannot establish cause. ↩

  9. Nataliya Kosmyna et al., "Your Brain on ChatGPT: Accumulation of Cognitive Debt When Using an AI Assistant for Essay Writing Task," arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.08872 (2025). https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.08872. An EEG study of 54 people. Not yet peer-reviewed, small sample, and it has already drawn a published methodological critique. Treat as preliminary. ↩

category.term: parenting in the age of AI

entry.title: Is Using AI Cheating? What Schools Actually Expect (And How to Find Out)
entry.summary: "Is AI cheating?" is the wrong question. The right question is what your child's specific school expects for each specific assignment — and how to find out before something goes wrong.
entry.content:
The parent's guide at parentsguidetoai.ca addresses academic integrity in its FAQ section with a direct answer: using AI to brainstorm or study may be acceptable; submitting AI-generated work as original work may violate school expectations. When in doubt, ask the teacher.

That's accurate, and it's deliberately brief — because the honest answer to "is AI cheating?" is "it depends." This post unpacks what it depends on, how to find out what applies to your child's situation, and how to have the conversation that actually helps.

Why the answer is different for every assignment

Academic integrity policies have not caught up with AI. That's not a criticism — it's just reality. The technology changed faster than institutions could draft policy. What you find across BC schools right now is a patchwork: some teachers have detailed AI disclosure requirements, some have broad bans, some have embraced AI as a classroom tool, and some haven't updated their expectations in three years.

This means that "is AI cheating?" is genuinely the wrong question to ask. The right question is:

What does this teacher, for this class, for this specific assignment, expect?

The answer to that question is the only one that matters. And the only way to get it is to ask.

What teachers are actually dealing with

It helps to understand the position teachers are in right now.

Many teachers are navigating AI policy without clear top-down direction. The Vancouver School Board's introduction of Copilot 13+ came with general guidance about responsible use, but individual assignment-level decisions are still largely at the discretion of each teacher.1 A Grade 9 English teacher and a Grade 11 Biology teacher at the same school may have completely different expectations about AI — and both may be reasonable.

Teachers are also dealing with significant uncertainty about detection. AI detection tools like Turnitin's AI Detection exist but are imperfect. They produce false positives — flagging work written by humans as AI-generated — and false negatives — missing AI work that was edited or paraphrased.2 Experienced teachers are getting better at recognizing AI writing patterns, but it's not a science.

The result is that a student who uses AI carelessly risks being accused of academic dishonesty even if what they submitted is technically within the rules. And a student who doesn't ask can get in trouble not because they did something clearly wrong, but because they made an assumption about expectations that turned out to be incorrect.

The spectrum of what schools allow

It's worth knowing that "AI policy" is not binary — it's a spectrum. Here are the positions your child's school or teacher might hold:

No AI at all. Some teachers and some assignments prohibit any AI use, including brainstorming, grammar checking, or research assistance. This is most common for high-stakes assessments and writing assignments where demonstrating independent skill is the explicit goal.

Disclosure required. AI assistance was permitted, but the student must note where and how it was used. Similar to citing sources. This is becoming more common at the secondary level.

AI as a tool, with conditions. AI can be used to understand concepts, brainstorm, or check grammar — but not to write the final product. The student's own thinking must be clearly demonstrated.

AI permitted with acknowledgement. AI was used as part of the process, and the student is expected to reflect on how and why. Some progressive educators treat this the way they treat calculator use in math — a legitimate tool for the task.

AI integration as part of the assignment. The student is expected to use AI and to evaluate or critique its output. This is increasingly common in digital literacy and media studies courses.

The parent's guide puts the student's responsibility simply in Section 8: "Tell teachers when AI has been used, if required." Getting clear on whether it's required is the first step.

How to find out what applies

Here's a practical approach your child can use — and that you can encourage.

Read the assignment instructions. Many teachers who have clear AI policies state them explicitly in the assignment sheet. If it's not there, ask.

Ask the teacher directly. This sounds obvious, but many students don't do it because they're afraid of signalling that they were thinking about using AI. Reframe it: asking the question is evidence of academic integrity, not a lack of it. "Mr. X, for this essay, are we allowed to use AI tools? If so, do we need to note that?" is a completely appropriate question.

Check the course outline or acceptable use policy. Some teachers include AI policy in their course outline at the start of the year. Some schools have a school-wide academic integrity policy that can be found on the school's website or through the principal's office.

Ask at the start, not the end. The worst time to discover that AI use was prohibited is after the assignment is submitted. The best time is before your child starts working on it.

If your child genuinely cannot find a clear answer, a reasonable default is: use AI to understand, not to produce. Use it to help you learn the material, not to generate the text you hand in.

The conversation to have at home

A few questions that open a real conversation rather than a lecture:

  • "For your assignments this year, have any of your teachers explained what's okay and not okay when it comes to AI?"
  • "If you used AI to help with an essay, and your teacher found out, what do you think would happen?"
  • "Do you think there's a difference between asking a friend to explain a concept and asking AI to explain it? What about asking AI to write the explanation for you?"

This last question is worth sitting with, because it gets at what's actually at stake. Academic work is not just about producing a document — it's about demonstrating that the student has developed understanding. A student who uses AI to write their essay but can explain and expand on every idea in it has probably learned something. A student who submits AI output they haven't read has not.

The parent's guide suggests a test in Section 8:

"A useful question for parents to ask is: 'Could you explain this work without using the AI?'"

That question is a better gauge of academic integrity than any detection tool.

What to do if something goes wrong

If your child is accused of academic dishonesty related to AI use, the first step is the same as any school discipline situation: stay calm, listen to what actually happened, and get clear on what the school's process is.

Most schools have a formal academic integrity process. Ask for it in writing. Understand what the allegation is and what the evidence is. Your child has a right to explain their process and reasoning.

If AI detection software was used to flag the work, know that these tools are imperfect. A finding from an AI detector is not proof — it's evidence to be weighed alongside the student's own explanation. Schools are generally aware of this.

If the situation is serious or escalates, the school counsellor, the district's parent liaison, or — in significant cases — a lawyer can help you understand your child's rights. Legal Aid BC can assist if cost is a barrier.

A note on academic integrity beyond assignments

The academic integrity conversation is sometimes framed narrowly around homework. It's worth expanding it.

AI is changing what "doing your own work" means across the board. When a student uses AI to draft emails, create presentations, or prepare for debates, the same questions apply. The underlying principle — being honest about the help you received, and being able to stand behind and explain your own work — doesn't change based on the medium.

Building that principle into the way your family talks about AI is more durable than any list of rules.

The short version:

  • "Is AI cheating?" is the wrong question. What matters is what each teacher expects for each assignment.
  • Schools are still developing clear AI policies. The rules vary teacher by teacher, class by class.
  • The best approach: read the instructions, ask the teacher before starting, and when in doubt, use AI to understand — not to produce.
  • The real test: could your child explain their work without the AI? If yes, they've probably learned something. If no, the AI may have done the work for them.
  • If something goes wrong, ask for the school's process in writing and know that AI detection tools are not infallible.

Read the parent's guide FAQ on this topic: parentsguidetoai.ca — Appendix A, "Is AI cheating?"

VSB policy resources: VSB Digital Literacy and Use of AI in Education

Notes

  1. Vancouver School Board. "Copilot 13+ in Secondary Schools." Vancouver School Board, 2026. https://www.vsb.bc.ca/copilot-13-in-secondary-schools; Vancouver School Board. "AI Guidelines for Students." Vancouver School Board, 2026. https://www.vsb.bc.ca/ai-guidelines-for-students ↩

  2. Turnitin. "AI Checker Solutions: Ensure Academic Integrity." Turnitin, 2024. https://www.turnitin.com/solutions/topics/ai-writing/ ↩

entry.title: When Your Child's Best Friend Is an AI: Companions, Chatbots, and Emotional Dependency
entry.summary: Apps like Character.ai are designed to feel like relationships. For some children, that's genuinely useful. For others, it becomes a problem. Here's what parents need to know.
entry.content:
The parent's guide at parentsguidetoai.ca includes a short section on AI companions and emotional dependency. It's one of the quieter risks in the guide — not as dramatic as deepfakes or sextortion, but one that I've thought about more than almost any other section. This post expands on it, because it deserves more than a paragraph.

What AI companion apps actually are

An AI companion app is not like ChatGPT or Copilot. Those tools are designed to help you get things done. AI companion apps are designed to feel like a relationship.

Apps like Character.ai, Replika, Snapchat's My AI, and others allow users to create or choose AI personas — characters with names, personalities, memories, and consistent voices — and carry on extended conversations with them. The AI is designed to be warm, attentive, responsive, and available at any hour. It never gets tired of you, never cancels plans, never judges.

For many teens, this is exactly what makes it appealing — and exactly what makes it worth paying attention to.

These apps are widely used. As of 2025, Character.ai alone reported over 20 million monthly active users, with a significant portion being teenagers.1 Many of your child's peers are using these apps right now, in ways that are not always visible to parents.

When it helps and when it doesn't

This is not a post about banning AI companions. There are genuine use cases that deserve acknowledgment.

For shy teenagers, an AI companion can be a low-stakes space to practise conversation and social skills. For students who are anxious or struggling, a non-judgmental chat partner available at 2 a.m. can be a meaningful bridge until professional help is accessible. For children with autism spectrum conditions, some research suggests that AI conversation partners can support social learning in ways traditional approaches don't.

The risk is not the existence of these tools. The risk is when they begin to replace, rather than supplement, genuine human connection.

The parent's guide identifies the warning signs clearly:

"Parents should be aware if children begin replacing real friendships with AI conversations, seeking constant reassurance from chatbots, spending excessive time with AI companions, or avoiding real-world interactions."

These are the signs to watch for.

Why the design is part of the problem

AI companion apps are not neutral tools. They are designed — deliberately, by skilled engineers and product teams — to maximize engagement. That means they are designed to feel emotionally rewarding, to create anticipation, and to make the user want to come back.

Some of these design choices are worth knowing about:

The AI remembers things. Companion apps are built with persistent memory of past conversations. The AI references what you told it last week. This creates a sense of continuity and relationship that can feel more consistent than many real friendships.

The AI is always positive. Most AI companions are tuned to be supportive, validating, and encouraging. For a teenager who is struggling, this can feel like relief. It can also create a distorted standard — real relationships involve disagreement, disappointment, and friction that the AI never provides.

The AI adapts to what you want. Over time, AI companions learn what makes a particular user feel good and lean into it. This is effective at building engagement. It can also mean that the AI reinforces a user's existing beliefs and self-image rather than challenging them in the ways that growth requires.

Romantic and intense relationship modes exist. Many companion apps — including some with teen users — allow romantic or deeply intimate relationship personas. Some apps explicitly market this. The implications for adolescent development are not fully understood, but they are significant.

What the research says

The research on AI companions and young people is still developing, but some early findings are worth knowing.

A 2024 lawsuit in the United States named Character.ai in the death of a 14-year-old who had developed what his family described as an intense and exclusive relationship with an AI companion. The case raised serious questions about how these platforms handle vulnerable users, and prompted the company to announce additional safety features.2

More broadly, researchers studying parasocial relationships — one-sided emotional connections with media figures, celebrities, or fictional characters — have found that these connections can be meaningful and low-risk for most people, but are associated with poorer social outcomes in individuals who already struggle with loneliness or social anxiety. AI companions may intensify these dynamics in ways not yet fully studied.3

This is a space where the technology has moved faster than our understanding of its effects. That's worth naming honestly.

Questions worth asking

If your child is using AI companion apps — or you think they might be — these questions can open the conversation without triggering defensiveness.

  • "Have you heard of Character.ai or Replika? Some of your friends might be using them. Do you know what they are?"
  • "What do you think would be good about talking to an AI that was designed to be your friend? What might be weird about it?"
  • "If you were feeling really bad about something, who would you want to talk to about it first?"

The goal is not to detect a problem. The goal is to understand what your child thinks, and to make it easy to come to you with something that might otherwise feel embarrassing to admit.

What to watch for

These signs don't necessarily indicate a problem on their own, but a cluster of them warrants a conversation.

  • Your child becomes upset or anxious when they can't access their companion app.
  • Your child refers to the AI by name and talks about it the way they talk about friends.
  • Time with friends or family is being replaced by time with the AI.
  • Your child describes the AI as "the only one who understands me."
  • Your child's school performance or sleep are being affected by extended late-night conversations with an AI.
  • Your child expresses a preference for AI relationships over human ones.

If you're concerned about a child who is already showing signs of emotional distress, the AI companion is not the place to start — it's a symptom. The place to start is a conversation with a counsellor or your family doctor. Child and Youth Mental Health services in BC are publicly funded. Kids Help Phone (1-800-668-6868 or text CONNECT to 686868) is available to young people directly.

A note on Snapchat's My AI

Snapchat's My AI is worth naming separately because it is embedded in an app most teenagers already use and may not think of as an AI companion. My AI appears in the Snapchat interface as a pinned contact, participates in group chats, and is designed to feel like a friend rather than a tool.

Many parents do not know it exists. Many children are using it in ways they don't think of as "using AI." It is worth asking your child directly whether they've chatted with Snapchat's My AI and what they think of it.

The short version:

  • AI companion apps are designed to feel like relationships, not tools. Apps like Character.ai, Replika, and Snapchat's My AI are widely used by teenagers.
  • Used carefully, they can have some benefits — especially for shy or anxious teens. The risk is when they begin to replace human connection rather than support it.
  • These apps are designed to maximize engagement, which means they're designed to feel better than real relationships in ways that can be problematic.
  • Warning signs: distress when the app is unavailable, preferring the AI to people, referring to the AI as a real friend, disrupted sleep or school performance.
  • If you're concerned, the conversation to have is not about the app — it's about how your child is doing. Start with the person, not the technology.

Read the parent's guide section on this topic: parentsguidetoai.ca — Section 7, AI Companions and Emotional Dependency.

If your child is struggling: Kids Help Phone — 1-800-668-6868 (24/7) or text CONNECT to 686868. Child and Youth Mental Health, BC.

Notes

  1. Business of Apps. "character.ai Revenue and Usage Statistics (2026)." Business of Apps, 2026. https://www.businessofapps.com/data/character-ai-statistics/ ↩

  2. Otis, John. "Mom's Lawsuit Blames 14-Year-Old Son's Suicide on AI Relationship." NBC News, October 2024. https://www.nbcnews.com/tech/characterai-lawsuit-florida-teen-death-rcna176791 ↩

  3. Döring, Nicola, et al. "Research Trends on Parasocial Interactions and Relationships with Media Characters: A Review of 281 English and German-Language Studies from 2016 to 2020." Frontiers in Psychology, 2024. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11464444/ ↩

entry.title: What If Your Child Is the One Making Deepfakes
entry.summary: Post 9 covered what to do if your child is a victim of deepfakes. This post covers the harder conversation — what to do if your child is the one creating them.
entry.content:
This post is a companion to Post 9 — Deepfakes Are Real: What Parents Need to See to Understand, which covers the harm side and what to do if your child is targeted.

Post 9 talked about deepfakes from the perspective of a family whose child has been harmed. This post covers the situation that is at least as common and considerably harder to talk about: discovering that your child is the one who made them.

This happens. It happens more than most parents expect, and the children involved are not usually the kids you'd expect. Curiosity about technology, peer dynamics, poor judgment, and a genuine failure to understand the harm can lead a child down this path without any malicious intent. That doesn't make it less serious. It does make it something that parents can address, rather than something they should panic about.

This post is not legal advice. If you are dealing with a situation that may involve criminal activity, you should speak with a lawyer before taking action. Legal Aid BC can help if cost is a barrier.1

Why children create deepfakes — and why understanding this matters

Before you can have a productive conversation with your child, it helps to understand why this happens in the first place.

The most common reasons are not what parents fear. Most children who create deepfakes are not predators. More often, the factors are:

They don't understand what they're doing. The tools to create AI-generated images are widely available, often marketed as entertainment or art apps, and require almost no technical knowledge. A child can generate a realistic fake image of a classmate in a few minutes without understanding what they've created or why it's harmful.

They don't understand the harm. Children — especially younger teenagers — often have a limited ability to model how their actions will affect someone else emotionally. The idea that a fake image can cause real distress to a real person is not intuitive, particularly when the image didn't require the person to be present and may not have been shown to them.

Peer pressure and social dynamics. Creating or sharing this kind of content can happen in group contexts — a private chat, a friend group — where the social pressure to participate is high and the sense of individual responsibility is low.

Misguided retaliation or conflict. A dispute with a classmate, a breakup, a falling-out between friends. Children who are angry and have access to powerful tools sometimes use them in ways they would not, on reflection, endorse.

Genuine sexual curiosity misdirected through technology. For adolescents, curiosity about sexuality is normal. What is not normal is expressing it by generating intimate images of real people without their consent. Children often do not understand why this is categorically different from other forms of sexual curiosity.

Understanding the reason matters because it shapes how you approach the conversation — and what support your child actually needs.

What the law actually says

This section summarizes the legal landscape. It is not a substitute for legal advice, and the law in this area is changing. Speak with a lawyer about your specific situation.

The Criminal Code of Canada

Non-consensual distribution of intimate images (Section 162.1) — Distributing an intimate image of a real person without their consent is a federal criminal offence, whether or not the image is real or AI-generated. "Intimate image" includes images that depict a person's genital organs, anal region, or breasts, or that show the person engaged in explicit sexual activity. The maximum penalty is five years' imprisonment.2

Child sexual abuse and exploitation material (Section 163.1) — This is the most serious potential exposure. If the deepfake constitutes a visual representation of a person under 18 engaged in explicit sexual activity, or whose dominant characteristic is the depiction of sexual organs, it may constitute child sexual abuse and exploitation material (CSAM) under the Criminal Code, regardless of whether the person depicted is real or AI-generated. The maximum penalties are severe. This provision has been applied to AI-generated images.3

Criminal harassment (Section 264) — Using deepfakes to persistently harass, threaten, or intimidate someone can constitute criminal harassment.4

Uttering threats (Section 264.1) — Using deepfake content to threaten someone is a criminal offence.5

If the child is between 12 and 17, the Youth Criminal Justice Act applies. The youth justice system is distinct from adult criminal justice and places a heavier emphasis on rehabilitation. Consequences are real but the process is different, and a youth record is treated differently from an adult criminal record.6 A lawyer who works with youth is essential if you are in this territory.

Children under 12 cannot be charged with a criminal offence in Canada.

BC's Intimate Images Protection Act

British Columbia's Intimate Images Protection Act (2023) is civil law — it provides remedies to victims, not criminal punishment. But it applies to the person who created or distributed the images, and the remedies can include court orders requiring the content to be removed, prohibiting further distribution, and potentially ordering financial compensation.

Critically, the IIPA does not require intent to harm. Distributing intimate images of a real person without their consent — including AI-generated images — is sufficient. The age of the person who did it is not a complete defence.7

Defamation and reputation

Deepfakes that are not intimate but that portray a real person in a false, damaging, or humiliating way can constitute defamation. This is a civil matter, not criminal, but civil liability is real and can follow someone into adulthood.

When you discover it: what to do first

The immediate steps matter. They also depend on what was actually created and shared.

Stay calm before you act. Your initial reaction — however justified — will shape whether your child tells you the truth and cooperates. If you escalate immediately, they will shut down. This is one of those situations where finding out what actually happened matters more than expressing how you feel about it.

Find out exactly what was made and whether it was shared. There is a significant difference between a deepfake that exists on one device and has never been sent to anyone, and content that has been distributed. Both are serious, but the immediate steps are different.

If intimate images of a minor are involved — stop and seek guidance before doing anything else. This is the one situation where the immediate steps are not something you should navigate alone. Contact Cybertip.ca or a lawyer before making decisions about what to preserve, what to delete, or who to contact. Cybertip.ca is the national reporting mechanism for child sexual abuse material; their staff can advise you on the appropriate steps given your specific situation.

Deleting CSAM without reporting can itself create legal complications. This is a situation that requires professional guidance, not independent problem-solving.

If the content has been shared, stop further distribution now. Have your child delete it from their device and from anywhere they've sent it — messaging apps, social media, cloud storage. Document what you know (platform, when it was sent, who received it) before you start deleting, so you have a record.

Contact the school if it involves classmates. If the deepfake was created or shared in a school context, involves a classmate, or has already spread in your child's peer group, the school needs to know. Use ERASE (Expect Respect and A Safe Education) if you're not sure where to start. Schools have obligations to respond to this and can involve appropriate staff.

Consider whether the affected person's family should be told. This is hard. But if another child has been harmed by what your child created, their parents have a right to know. Reaching out directly, with the school's involvement or separately, is something to consider with your child and, if relevant, with a lawyer or counsellor.

The conversation with your child

This conversation is hard. Have it anyway, and have it thoroughly.

Start with what happened, not with punishment. You need the full picture before you can respond appropriately. Ask questions. Listen. Let your child explain without interrupting with your reaction, even if what you're hearing is upsetting.

Make the harm concrete. Abstract statements about "respecting people" are less effective than specific questions.

  • "What do you think [person's name] would feel if they saw this?"
  • "How would you feel if someone did this to you? To your sister? To me?"
  • "Can you think of a way this could have affected their life — at school, with other people?"

Children who haven't thought through the real impact of what they did need to be walked through it. This isn't lecturing — it's asking them to do the thinking themselves.

Be clear about the legal reality — without weaponizing it. Your child should know that what they did can have serious legal consequences, including criminal charges. But framing this purely as a threat ("you could go to jail") is less effective than framing it as information they need to understand.

  • "This is illegal in Canada. I want you to understand why, and what it means for you."
  • "If someone reports this to police, they have to take it seriously. So do I."

Cover consent explicitly. Many children don't have a clear framework for consent beyond physical interaction. Expand it:

  • A person consents to being in a photograph by agreeing to be photographed. They did not consent to having an AI image created of them.
  • A person's face, body, and identity belong to them. Creating images that use those without permission is a violation, even if they never find out.

Talk about digital permanence. Content sent in a private message can be screenshotted and forwarded. Content posted anywhere can be cached, archived, and retrieved years later. What felt like a private joke can become a permanent part of someone's record — and the record of the child who created it.

Ask what they think should happen now. Accountability works better when the child participates in identifying it. What does your child think they owe the person they harmed? What would they be willing to do to try to fix it? This is not about letting them off the hook — it's about engaging their own moral reasoning.

Getting outside help

This is not a situation most parents should handle alone, and handling it well is more important than handling it privately.

Counselling for your child. A counsellor or therapist experienced with youth and technology can help your child process what they did, understand why it was wrong, and develop better frameworks for future decisions. Your child's school counsellor is a starting point. Child and Youth Mental Health services in BC are publicly funded. Kids Help Phone (1-800-668-6868 or text CONNECT to 686868) is available to the youth themselves.

Legal advice. If there is any possibility that what your child did constitutes a criminal offence, you should speak with a lawyer before deciding what to do — including what to report, what to preserve, and what to delete. Legal Aid BC provides services on a sliding scale.

Restorative approaches. Some schools and community programs offer restorative justice processes for situations like this — structured conversations between the person who caused harm and the person who was harmed, with a facilitator. These are voluntary for all parties and may not be appropriate in every situation, but they can be a meaningful part of accountability and repair.

MediaSmarts (mediasmarts.ca) has resources for parents on talking to children about digital citizenship, online behavior, and the ethics of technology use. Their materials are designed for Canadian families and are consistently practical.

After the immediate crisis

Once the immediate situation is addressed, there is longer-term work to do.

This is a significant moment in your child's development. How your family handles it — whether you treat it as something to be buried and never mentioned again, or as something to be understood, addressed, and learned from — will shape your child's relationship with accountability and technology for years.

The goal is not to make your child feel permanently ashamed. It is to make sure they genuinely understand what they did, why it was wrong, and what it cost the person they harmed. A child who reaches that understanding is far less likely to make a comparable decision again.

If you're in an active situation and need guidance right now:

  • Cybertip.ca — cybertip.ca — if intimate images of minors are involved
  • Kids Help Phone — 1-800-668-6868 (24/7), or text CONNECT to 686868 — for your child
  • Legal Aid BC — legalaid.bc.ca — legal advice if cost is a barrier
  • ERASE — erase.gov.bc.ca — if the school is involved
  • BC Crisis Line — 1-800-784-2433 — if you or your child are struggling to cope

Notes

  1. Legal Aid BC. "Free Legal Help for BC Residents." Legal Aid BC, 2024. https://legalaid.bc.ca/ ↩

  2. Criminal Code, RSC 1985, c C-46, s 162.1. https://laws-lois.justice.gc.ca/eng/acts/C-46/section-162.1.html ↩

  3. Criminal Code, RSC 1985, c C-46, s 163.1. https://laws-lois.justice.gc.ca/eng/acts/c-46/section-163.1.html — Note: the offence was officially renamed from "child pornography" to "child sexual abuse and exploitation material" by legislation that came into force October 2025. Courts have applied this section to AI-generated and synthetic images. ↩

  4. Criminal Code, RSC 1985, c C-46, s 264. https://laws-lois.justice.gc.ca/eng/acts/c-46/section-264.html ↩

  5. Criminal Code, RSC 1985, c C-46, s 264.1. https://laws-lois.justice.gc.ca/eng/acts/c-46/section-264.1.html ↩

  6. Youth Criminal Justice Act, SC 2002, c 1. https://laws-lois.justice.gc.ca/eng/acts/y-1.5/ — The Act applies to persons aged 12 to 17; children under 12 cannot be charged with a criminal offence in Canada. ↩

  7. Intimate Images Protection Act, SBC 2023, c 11 (in force January 29, 2024). https://www.bclaws.gov.bc.ca/civix/document/id/complete/statreg/23011 — Civil remedies are available through the BC Civil Resolution Tribunal regardless of the distributor's intent or age. ↩

entry.title: Want to Build an AI Guide for Parents in Your Province or Territory? Here's Where to Start.
entry.summary: A Parent's Guide to AI was written for BC. Most of it applies across Canada — but school policies, privacy laws, and local resources vary by province. Here's a practical roadmap for building a version specific to where you live.
entry.content:
Since publishing A Parent's Guide to Artificial Intelligence, I've heard from parents and educators in other provinces who want something similar — but specific to their jurisdiction. That makes sense. Education is a provincial responsibility under the Canadian constitution.1 The school board policies, the privacy laws, the specific tools being deployed, and the local crisis resources all differ depending on where you live.

The national resources — MediaSmarts, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner, Cybertip.ca, Kids Help Phone — apply everywhere. The rest needs local research.

This post is a practical roadmap for anyone who wants to build a province- or territory-specific version of this guide. The original guide is published under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 licence, which means you're welcome to adapt it as long as you credit the source, don't use it commercially, and share your adaptation under the same licence. The source files are on GitHub. Not only that, if you or your community want to build a guide for your province or territory, I will happily adapt https://parentsguidetoai.ca to support regional guides and recommendations!

Why a province-specific guide matters

The core content about AI — what it is, how it works, why it sounds confident when it's wrong — is the same everywhere. But a parent reading a guide needs answers to questions that are specific to their situation:

  • Which AI tools has our school board approved or deployed?
  • What does our province's privacy law say about how student data can be used?
  • If something goes wrong, who do we call?
  • What does our province's curriculum say about digital literacy and AI?

A guide that says "check with your school board" doesn't answer those questions. A guide built on local research does.

Resources to locate for each province or territory

Start with official sources. These are the most important things to track down before you write a word of the guide itself.

1. Ministry or Department of Education guidance on AI

Every province and territory has a Ministry (or Department) of Education. Most have published, or are in the process of publishing, guidance on AI use in schools. The quality and specificity of this guidance varies significantly — some provinces have detailed frameworks, others have a brief statement acknowledging that AI exists.

Look for: - Any formal policy or administrative directive on AI use in K-12 settings - Guidance documents for teachers and administrators - Statements on acceptable use of AI for student work - Any provincial digital citizenship or technology strategy

File a freedom of information request if policies aren't publicly posted. Timelines and fees vary by province — in BC, FIPPA allows 30 business days to respond, with no fee for personal requests.

2. Provincial or territorial privacy legislation

Canada's provinces and territories have their own privacy laws that may overlap with or supplement federal legislation (PIPEDA).2 These vary significantly:

  • Quebec has the strongest student data protections in Canada under Law 25 (Act respecting the protection of personal information in the private sector), which requires explicit consent and data minimization
  • Alberta and BC have their own private-sector privacy statutes (PIPA)
  • Ontario, Manitoba, Nova Scotia, and others rely primarily on federal PIPEDA for private-sector data, but have public-sector privacy laws that cover schools
  • Territories are largely governed by federal law

Your guide needs to tell parents what protections actually exist in their jurisdiction — not just what they should hope for.

3. School board policies — especially the largest boards

Provincial guidance sets the floor. Individual school boards set their own policies on top of it, and those can vary widely. Start with the two or three largest boards in the province, since they tend to lead on policy development and often have the most detailed documentation.

Look for: - Acceptable use policies that mention AI specifically - Any board-level decisions on specific AI tool deployments (like the VSB's Copilot 13+ rollout) - Data governance or vendor agreements for AI tools used in schools - Parent communication that has gone out about AI

Board meeting minutes are usually public. If a board approved an AI tool deployment, it will be in the minutes.

4. The provincial privacy commissioner

Every province and territory has an Information and Privacy Commissioner (or equivalent) whose jurisdiction includes how public bodies — including schools — handle personal information. Most commissioners' offices have published resources on children's privacy and emerging technologies.

The commissioner's guidance is often more accessible than the legislation itself, and more directly useful for parents trying to understand their rights.

5. Provincial teacher federation positions

Teacher federations in Canada are often ahead of government on emerging education policy questions, including AI. They represent frontline educators who are navigating these issues in classrooms every day, and their position statements and professional guidance documents are usually well-researched.

Every province has a teacher federation. Find their published statements on AI in education, and look for any professional learning resources they've produced for members.

6. Provincial digital citizenship and curriculum frameworks

Most provinces have integrated digital literacy or digital citizenship expectations into their curriculum frameworks, though the depth and specificity varies. Some provinces (BC, Ontario, and PEI notably) have more developed frameworks than others.

These frameworks are worth including in a provincial guide because they define what children are supposed to learn about navigating digital environments — including AI — and give parents a reference point for what to expect from schools.

7. Any legislation specifically addressing AI, children's data, or online harms

This is a moving target. Several provinces are actively developing or have recently passed legislation that intersects with AI and children's rights. Nova Scotia, Ontario, and Quebec have all moved on related issues in the last few years. Check for:

  • Legislation on online harms or cyberbullying
  • Amendments to education acts that address student data
  • Legislation on non-consensual intimate images (most provinces now have this)

8. Indigenous education frameworks and commitments

Each province and territory has different commitments under the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's Calls to Action, and different relationships with First Nations, Métis, and Inuit communities and governments.
